fact: The tsunami that struck Fukushima Daiichi was approximately 40 feet (about 14-15 meters) tall
source: Tokyo Electric Power Company (TEPCO) and IAEA reports indicate the tsunami waves were approximately 14-15 meters (46-49 feet) at the plant site

fact: The backup diesel generators were located in the basement and were flooded by the tsunami
source: The Official Report of The Fukushima Nuclear Accident Independent Investigation Commission (NAIIC), National Diet of Japan (2012)

fact: The reactors shut down automatically (SCRAMmed) when the earthquake struck, as designed
source: IAEA, 'The Fukushima Daiichi Accident: Report by the Director General' (2015)

fact: Residual decay heat from the fuel continued to generate massive heat after shutdown, eventually causing fuel meltdown without cooling
source: Nuclear Energy Agency (OECD/NEA), 'Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant Accident' — decay heat is a well-documented phenomenon in nuclear engineering; meltdowns in Units 1, 2, and 3 are confirmed by TEPCO

fact: Hydrogen gas was vented to relieve pressure and subsequently caused explosions at the reactor buildings
source: Investigation Committee on the Accident at the Fukushima Nuclear Power Stations, Interim Report (2011); hydrogen explosions occurred at Units 1, 3, and 4

fact: The decision to place backup generators below sea level (in basements vulnerable to flooding) was a critical design flaw that led to the disaster
source: The Fukushima Nuclear Accident Independent Investigation Commission (NAIIC), National Diet of Japan (2012), which concluded the disaster was 'man-made' due to foreseeable failures in safety planning
